content.full_text:
State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 807 · SACRAMENTO, 95804 · · · 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

Technical Assistance Administration · · · Executive Legal · · · Enforcement · · · Conflict of Interest
916) 322-5660          322-5901          322-6441          322-6444

May 10, 1979


Jeff L. B. Hulse
Planning Director
County of Nevada
Planning Department
205 Willow Valley Road
Nevada City, CA 95959

Dear Mr. Hulse:

This letter constitutes advice provided pursuant to
Government Code Section 83114 (b) and confirms the interim
advice which I provided previously.

The facts as I understand them are as follows: the
Board of Supervisors of Nevada County is preparing to amend
the county's general plan. The previous general plan was
designed to accommodate a population of 273,000 people. However, all three proposals before the Board of Supervisors for
amending the general plan are based on substantially lower
population projections. Therefore, minimum lot sizes under
all three proposals will be substantially increased over the
minimum lot size permitted under the current general plan.
Moreover, to the extent the general plan ultimately adopted
by the county differs significantly from current zoning ordinances, the ordinances will have to be amended to conform
to the general plan. The net effect of these proposals, in
differing degrees, will be that property which currently may
be subject to multiple subdivision will not be allowed to be
as heavily subdivided and in some instances may not be subdivided at all.

Mr. Jeff L. B. Hulse
May 10, 1979
                                                                    Page Two


     Government Code Section 87100 1/ provides:

     No public official at any level of state or
     local government shall make, participate in
     making or in any way attempt to use his official
     position to influence a governmental decision
     in which he knows or has reason to know he has
     a financial interest.

     Section 87103(b) provides that an official has a financial inter-
est in a governmental decision if any real property in which
he has an ownership, option or leasehold interest may foreseeably
be materially affected by the decision in a manner different
from its effect on the public generally.  See also Section 82033.
Enclosed please find a copy of Commission Regulation 18702 which
defines the term "material financial effect".

     In the instant case, you have indicated that Supervisors
Hunyada, Wilder, Covert, Rood, Supervisor-elect Hansen and several
planning commissioners have interests in real property in Nevada
County.

     The question to be resolved is, therefore, whether partici-
pation in all or part of the decision-making process regarding
the adoption of the general plan will have a foreseable material
financial effect on the interests in real property on the offi-
cials in question. Based on the information provided to us, we
think there are several areas in which the official should be
disqualified from participating.  First, the classification of
the property as urban, residential, rural, etc. will foreseeably
have a material financial effect on real property because these
decisions affect the way in which the property may be used.
Accordingly, an official may not make, participate in making
or use his official position to influence a decision regarding
the classification of his property.  In addition, since the
classification of property adjacent to or in the vicinity of an
official's property can directly affect the classification of
the official's property, the official should disqualify himself
from any involvement in the classification of such properties
as well.


_____________________________
1/  All statutory references are to the Government Code
unless otherwise noted.

Mr. Jeff L. B. Hulse
May 10, 1979                                                    Page Three


        Second, the value of real property will be substantially
affected by the degree to which it may be subdivided. In the
instant case, there are at least three alternative proposals
concerning the minimum lot size for properties included within
each type of land use classification. Based on the information
provided to us, we think it is foreseeable that each parcel of
real property in question will be materially affected by adoption
of at least one of the alternative minimum lot size proposals.
Because at least one of the alternative proposals will materially
affect each parcel of property in question, a decision to adopt
an alternative proposal necessarily will materially affect the
property since it prevents adoption of the other proposals.
Accordingly, each of the officials in question is prohibited
from making, participating in the making or using his official
position to influence the making of any decision regarding any
proposal concerning the minimum lot size of property in the same
classification as his property.

        2/ The following is a sample of the property holdings of some
of the officials in question.

        Supervisor Hunyada: 18 acres of unimproved property which
currently may not be subdivided into lots smaller than 5 acre parcels.
Depending on which proposal is ultimately adopted, the minimum
lot size would rise to between 10 and 30 acres. Supervisor
Hunyada also owns a grocery store in a rural area which will be
affected by decisions regarding minimum lot size in adjoining
areas.

        Supervisor Covert: 18 acres of property which currently is
subject to a 1.5 acre minimum. Depending on the proposal ultimately
adopted, the minimum lot size would rise to between 20 and 40 acres.

        Supervisor Wilder: 40.5 acres which currently is subject to
a two acre minimum. Depending on the proposal ultimately adopted,
the minimum lot size could drop to one acre or rise to three acres.

        Supervisor Rood: 21 acres which is currently subject to a
1.5 acre minimum. Depending on the proposal ultimately adopted,
the minimum lot size could drop to 1 acre or rise to 3 acres.

        3/ This would not be our conclusion if any of the officials
in question had property in the heavily urbanized portions of the
county. None of the alternative proposals regarding such property
will substantially change current zoning regulation. Therefore,
it is not foreseeable that adoption of any of the alternatives
will materially affect such property.

Mr. Jeff L. B. Hulse
May 10, 1979                                                Page Four


     Thus, if an official's property is classified "rural resi-
dential" he may not participate in decisions concerning the
minimum lot size of property classified "rural residential".
However, he may participate in decisions regarding the minimum
lot size of property on other classifications such as "urban
residential" or "commercial".

     Commission Regulation 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18700(d)(2)
provides, however, that a disqualified official may appear before
his agency as a member of the general public on matters related
solely to his own personal interests.  Thus, any of the dis-
qualified officials in this case may appear before the Planning
Commission or the Board of Supervisors to testify regarding the
classification or minimum lot size of his own property so long
as he is afforded no greater opportunity to present his testi-
mony than any other member of the general public.

     It is my understanding that proceeding along the lines
set forth above will not cause either the Planning Commission
or the Board of Supervisors to operate without a quorum.  However,
if this should occur, you should reconstitute the quorum by
following the procedures set forth in the Matthew Hudson opinion,
which I have enclosed.

     If you have any further questions, please contact me.

                                                  Sincerely,


                                                  [illegible]
                                                  Ted Prim, Chief
                                                  Conflicts of Interest Division

ever, all three proposals before the Board of Supervisors for

pation in all or part of the decision-making process regarding

cials in question. Based on the information provided to us, we
think there are several areas in which the official should be

the property as urban, residential, rural, etc. will foreseeably

Accordingly, an official may not make, participate in making,
or use his official position to influence a decision regarding
the classification of his property. In addition, since the
classification of property adjacent to or in the vicinity of an
official's property can directly affect the classification of
the official's property, the official should disqualify himself
from any involvement in the classification of such properties
as well.

_______________________

1/ All statutory references are to the Government Code
   unless otherwise noted.

Mr. Jeff L. B. Hulse                                      Page Three


     Second, the value of real property will be substantially
affected by the degree to which it may be subdivided. In the
instant case, there are at least three alternative proposals
concerning the minimum lot size for properties included within
each type of land use classification. Based on the information
provided to us, we think it is foreseeable that each parcel of
real property in question will be materially affected by adoption
of at least one of the alternative minimum lot size proposals.2/
Because at least one of the alternative proposals will materially
affect each parcel of property in question, any decision to adopt
an alternative proposal necessarily will materially affect the
property since it prevents adoption of the other proposals.
Accordingly, each of the officials in question is prohibited
from making, participating in the making or using his official
position to influence the making of any decision regarding any
proposal concerning the minimum lot size of property in the same
classification as his property.3/


2/ The following is a sample of the property holdings of some
of the officials in question.

     Supervisor Hunyada: 18 acres of unimproved property which
currently may not be subdivided into less than 5 acre parcels.
Depending on which proposal is ultimately adopted, the minimum
lot size would rise to between 10 and 30 acres. Supervisor
Hunyada also owns a grocery store in a rural area which will be
affected by decisions regarding minimum lot size in adjoining

     Supervisor Covert: 18 acres of property which currently is
subject to a 1.5 acre minimum. Depending on the proposal ultimately
adopted, the minimum lot size would rise to between 20 and 40 acres.

     Supervisor Wilder: 40.5 acres which currently is subject to
a two acre minimum. Depending on the proposal ultimately adopted,
the minimum lot size could drop to one acre or rise to three acres.

     Supervisor Rood: 21 acres which is currently subject to a
1.5 acre minimum. Depending on the proposal ultimately adopted,
the minimum lot size could drop to 1 acre or rise to 3 acres.

     3/ This would not be our conclusion if any of the officials
in question had property in the heavily urbanized portions of the
county. None of the alternative proposals regarding such property
will substantially change current zoning regulation. Therefore,
it is not foreseeable that adoption of any of the alternatives
will materially affect such property.

Mr. Jeff L. B. Hulse
Page Four


Thus, if an official's property is classified "rural residential" he may not participate in decisions concerning the minimum lot size of property classified "rural residential". However, he may participate in decisions regarding the minimum lot size of property on other classifications such as "urban residential" or "commercial".

Commission Regulation 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18700(d)(2) provides, however, that a disqualified official may appear before his agency as a member of the general public on matters related solely to his own personal interests. Thus, any of the disqualified officials in this case may appear before the Planning Commission or the Board of Supervisors to testify regarding the classification or minimum lot size of his own property so long as he is afforded no greater opportunity to present his testimony than any other member of the general public.

It is my understanding that proceeding along the lines set forth above will not cause either the Planning Commission or the Board of Supervisors to operate without a quorum. However, if this should occur, you should reconstitute the quorum by following the procedures set forth in the Matthew Hudson opinion, which I have enclosed.

If you have any further questions, please contact me.

Sincerely,


Ted Prim, Chief
Conflicts of Interest Division

TP:ma
Enclosures

State of California

Memorandum

To : Commissioners Houston, Japan,
     McAndrews, Quinn and Remcho

Date : May 3, 1979

From : FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
       Ted Prim

Subject: Advice letter to Jeff L. B. Hulse, Planning Director,
         County of Nevada Planning Department

Attached is an advice letter which confirms interim
staff advice which was provided previously. Since the
background information for this letter consists of several
telephone calls and voluminous documents outlining the
alternative general plans, no background materials accompany
the letter. However, I have attempted to include all
relevant considerations in the letter, e.g., footnote 2.

If you have any questions or comments regarding the
proposed response, please contact me no later than 2:00 p.m.
on Wednesday, May 9, 1979.

sections.question_synthetic: Whether Nevada County supervisors and planning commissioners with real property interests in the county should be disqualified from participating in decisions regarding the adoption of a new general plan that would affect minimum lot sizes and property classifications.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: Officials must disqualify themselves from participating in decisions regarding property classifications and minimum lot sizes that would materially affect their own property holdings, though they may appear as members of the general public to testify about their own property interests. Officials may participate in decisions regarding other property classifications where they hold no interest.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: No explicit 'QUESTION' or 'CONCLUSION' section headers in document, but the question is synthesized from the problem statement in the body and conclusion from the analytical advice provided. Document contains detailed analysis of Government Code Sections 87100 and 87103(b) with specific examples of supervisor property holdings affected by three alternative proposals.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Whether Nevada County supervisors and planning commissioners with real property interests in the county should be disqualified from participating in decisions regarding the adoption of a new general plan that would affect minimum lot sizes and property classifications.

CONCLUSION: Officials must disqualify themselves from participating in decisions regarding property classifications and minimum lot sizes that would materially affect their own property holdings, though they may appear as members of the general public to testify about their own property interests. Officials may participate in decisions regarding other property classifications where they hold no interest.

embedding.summary: FPPC advice letter to Nevada County Planning Director regarding conflict of interest disqualifications for county supervisors and planning commissioners who own real property that would be materially affected by the county's proposed general plan amendments changing minimum lot sizes and property classifications.
